Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## PROGRESSIVE DISTILLATION FOR FAST SAMPLING OF DIFFUSION MODELS

## **Tim Salimans & Jonathan Ho**

Google Research, Brain team {salimans,jonathanho}@google.com

## ABSTRACT

Diffusion models have recently shown great promise for generative modeling, outperforming GANs on perceptual quality and autoregressive models at density estimation. A remaining downside is their slow sampling time: generating high quality samples takes many hundreds or thousands of model evaluations. Here we make two contributions to help eliminate this downside: First, we present new parameterizations of diffusion models that provide increased stability when using few sampling steps. Second, we present a method to distill a trained deterministic diffusion sampler, using many steps, into a new diffusion model that takes half as many sampling steps. We then keep progressively applying this distillation procedure to our model, halving the number of required sampling steps each time. On standard image generation benchmarks like CIFAR-10, ImageNet, and LSUN, we start out with state-of-the-art samplers taking as many as 8192 steps, and are able to distill down to models taking as few as 4 steps without losing much perceptual quality; achieving, for example, a FID of 3.0 on CIFAR-10 in 4 steps. Finally, we show that the full progressive distillation procedure does not take more time than it takes to train the original model, thus representing an efficient solution for generative modeling using diffusion at both train and test time.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Diffusion models (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Song & Ermon, 2019; Ho et al., 2020) are an emerging class of generative models that has recently delivered impressive results on many standard generative modeling benchmarks. These models have achieved ImageNet generation results outperforming BigGAN-deep and VQ-VAE-2 in terms of FID score and classification accuracy score (Ho et al., 2021; Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021), and they have achieved likelihoods outperforming autoregressive image models (Kingma et al., 2021; Song et al., 2021b). They have also succeeded in image super-resolution (Saharia et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021) and image inpainting (Song et al., 2021c), and there have been promising results in shape generation (Cai et al., 2020), graph generation (Niu et al., 2020), and text generation (Hoogeboom et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021).

A major barrier remains to practical adoption of diffusion models: sampling speed. While sampling can be accomplished in relatively few steps in strongly conditioned settings, such as text-tospeech (Chen et al., 2021) and image super-resolution (Saharia et al., 2021), or when guiding the sampler using an auxiliary classifier (Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021), the situation is substantially different in settings in which there is less conditioning information available. Examples of such settings are unconditional and standard class-conditional image generation, which currently require hundreds or thousands of steps using network evaluations that are not amenable to the caching optimizations of other types of generative models (Ramachandran et al., 2017).

In this paper, we reduce the sampling time of diffusion models by orders of magnitude in unconditional and class-conditional image generation, which represent the setting in which diffusion models have been slowest in previous work. We present a procedure to distill the behavior of a _N_ -step DDIM sampler (Song et al., 2021a) for a pretrained diffusion model into a new model with _N/_ 2 steps, with little degradation in sample quality. In what we call _progressive distillation_ , we repeat this distillation procedure to produce models that generate in as few as 4 steps, still maintaining sample quality competitive with state-of-the-art models using thousands of steps.

If **x** ˆ _θ_ ( **z** _t_ ) satisfies mild smoothness conditions, the error introduced by numerical integration of the probability flow ODE is guaranteed to vanish as the number of integration steps grows infinitely large, i.e. _N →∞_ . This leads to a trade-off in practice between the accuracy of the numerical integration, and hence the quality of the produced samples from our model, and the time needed to produce these samples. So far, most models in the literature have needed hundreds or thousands of integration steps to produce their highest quality samples, which is prohibitive for many practical applications of generative modeling. Here, we therefore propose a method to distill these accurate, but slow, ODE integrators into much faster models that are still very accurate. This idea is visualized in Figure 1, and described in detail in the next section.

## 3 PROGRESSIVE DISTILLATION

To make diffusion models more efficient at sampling time, we propose _progressive distillation_ : an algorithm that iteratively halves the number of required sampling steps by distilling a slow teacher diffusion model into a faster student model. Our implementation of progressive distillation stays very close to the implementation for training the original diffusion model, as described by e.g. Ho et al. (2020). Algorithm 1 and Algorithm 2 present diffusion model training and progressive distillation side-by-side, with the relative changes in progressive distillation highlighted in green.

We start the progressive distillation procedure with a teacher diffusion model that is obtained by training in the standard way. At every iteration of progressive distillation, we then initialize the student model with a copy of the teacher, using both the same parameters and same model definition. Like in standard training, we then sample data from the training set and add noise to it, before forming the training loss by applying the student denoising model to this noisy data **z** _t_ . predicting a weighted average of possible **x** values, which produces a blurry prediction. By making sharper predictions, the student model can make faster progress during sampling.

After running distillation to learn a student model taking _N_ sampling steps, we can repeat the procedure with _N/_ 2 steps: The student model then becomes the new teacher, and a new student model is initialized by making a copy of this model.

Unlike our procedure for training the original model, we always run progressive distillation in discrete time: we sample this discrete time such that the highest time index corresponds to a signal-tonoise ratio of zero, i.e. _α_ 1 = 0, which exactly matches the distribution of input noise **z** 1 _∼N_ ( **0** _,_ **I** ) that is used at test time. We found this to work slightly better than starting from a non-zero signalto-noise ratio as used by e.g. Ho et al. (2020), both for training the original model as well as when performing progressive distillation.

4 DIFFUSION MODEL PARAMETERIZATION AND TRAINING LOSS

In this section, we discuss how to parameterize the denoising model **x** ˆ _θ_ , and how to specify the reconstruction loss weight _w_ ( _λt_ ). We assume a standard _variance-preserving_ diffusion process for which _σt_[2][=][1] _[−][α] t_[2][.][This][is][without][loss][of][generalization,][as][shown][by][(Kingma][et][al.,][2021,] appendix G): different specifications of the diffusion process, such as the _variance-exploding_ specification, can be considered equivalent to this specification, up to rescaling of the noisy latents **z** _t_ . We use a cosine schedule _αt_ = cos(0 _._ 5 _πt_ ), similar to that introduced by Nichol & Dhariwal (2021).

(2020) and much of the following work choose to parameterize the denoising model through directly predicting _ϵ_ with a neural network ˆ _ϵθ_ ( **z** _t_ ), which implicitly sets ˆ **x** _θ_ ( **z** _t_ ) = _α_ 1 _t_[(] **[z]** _[t][−][σ][t][ϵ]_[ˆ] _[θ]_[(] **[z]** _[t]_[))][.] In this case, the training loss is also usually defined as mean squared error in the _ϵ_ -space:

Although this standard specification works well for training the original model, it is not well suited for distillation: when training the original diffusion model, and at the start of progressive distillation, the model is evaluated at a wide range of signal-to-noise ratios _αt_[2] _[/σ] t_[2][, but as distillation progresses] we increasingly evaluate at lower and lower signal-to-noise ratios. As the signal-to-noise ratio goes to zero, the effect of small changes in the neural network output ˆ _ϵθ_ ( **z** _t_ ) on the implied prediction in 1 **x** -space is increasingly amplified, since ˆ **x** _θ_ ( **z** _t_ ) = _αt_[(] **[z]** _[t][ −][σ][t][ϵ]_[ˆ] _[θ]_[(] **[z]** _[t]_[))][ divides by] _[ α][t][→]_[0][.][This is not] much of a problem when taking many steps, since the effect of early missteps is limited by clipping of the **z** _t_ iterates, and later updates can correct any mistakes, but it becomes increasingly important as we decrease the number of sampling steps. Eventually, if we distill all the way down to a single sampling step, the input to the model is only pure noise _ϵ_ , which corresponds to a signal-to-noise ratio of zero, i.e. _αt_ = 0 _, σt_ = 1. At this extreme, the link between _ϵ_ -prediction and **x** -prediction breaks down completely: observed data **z** _t_ = _ϵ_ is no longer informative of **x** and predictions ˆ _ϵθ_ ( **z** _t_ ) no longer implicitly predict **x** . Examining our reconstruction loss (equation 9), we see that the weighting function _w_ ( _λt_ ) gives zero weight to the reconstruction loss at this signal-to-noise ratio.

For distillation to work, we thus need to parameterize the diffusion model in a way for which the implied prediction ˆ **x** _θ_ ( **z** _t_ ) remains stable as _λt_ = log[ _αt_[2] _[/σ] t_[2][]][ varies.][We tried the following options,] and found all to work well with progressive distillation:

- Predicting **x** directly.

- Predicting both **x** and _ϵ_ , via separate output channelsˆ _{_ **x** ˜ _θ_ ( **z** _t_ ) _,_ ˜ _ϵθ_ ( **z** _t_ ) _}_ of the neural network, and then merging the predictions via **x** = _σt_[2] **[x]**[˜] _[θ]_[(] **[z]** _[t]_[)][+] _[α][t]_[(] **[z]** _[t][−][σ][t][ϵ]_[˜] _[θ]_[(] **[z]** _[t]_[))][,][thus] smoothly interpolating between predicting **x** directly and predicting via _ϵ_ .
- Predicting **v** _≡ αtϵ − σt_ **x** , which gives **x** ˆ = _αt_ **z** _t − σt_ **v** ˆ _θ_ ( **z** _t_ ), as we show in Appendix D.
In Section 5.1 we test all three parameterizations on training an original diffusion model (no distillation), and find them to work well there also.

In addition to determining an appropriate parameterization, we also need to decide on a reconstruction loss weighting _w_ ( _λt_ ). The setup of Ho et al. (2020) weights the reconstruction loss by the signal-to-noise ratio, implicitly gives a weight of zero to data with zero SNR, and is therefore not a suitable choice for distillation. We consider two alternative training loss weightings:

We examine both choices in our ablation study in Section 5.1, and find both to be good choices for training diffusion models. In practice, the choice of loss weighting also has to take into account how _αt, σt_ are sampled during training, as this sampling distribution strongly determines the weight the expected loss gives to each signal-to-noise ratio. Our results are for a cosine schedule _αt_ = cos(0 _._ 5 _πt_ ), where time is sampled uniformly from [0 _,_ 1]. In Figure 2 we visualize the resulting loss weightings, both including and excluding the effect of the cosine schedule.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

In this section we empirically validate the progressive distillation algorithm proposed in Section 3, as well as the parameterizations and loss weightings considered in Section 4. We consider various image generation benchmarks, with resolution varying from 32 _×_ 32 to 128 _×_ 128. All experiments use the cosine schedule _αt_ = cos(0 _._ 5 _πt_ ), and all models use a U-Net architecture similar to that introduced by Ho et al. (2020), but with BigGAN-style up- and downsampling (Brock et al., 2019), as used in the diffusion modeling setting by Nichol & Dhariwal (2021); Song et al. (2021c). Our training setup closely matches the open source code by Ho et al. (2020). Exact details are given in Appendix E.

We therefore proposed various alternative parameterizations of the denoising diffusion model that are stable under the progressive distillation procedure, as well as various weighting functions for the reconstruction error in **x** -space. Here, we perform a complete ablation experiment of all parameterizations and loss weightings considered in Section 4. For computational efficiency, and for comparisons to established methods in the literature, we use unconditional CIFAR-10 as the benchmark. We measure performance of undistilled models trained from scratch, to avoid introducing too many factors of variation into our analysis.

Table 1 lists the results of the ablation study. Overall results are fairly close across different parameterizations and loss weights. All proposed stable model specifications achieve excellent performance, with the exception of the combination of outputting _ϵ_ with the neural network and weighting the loss with the truncated SNR, which we find to be unstable. Both predicting **x** directly, as well as predicting **v** , or the combination ( _ϵ,_ **x** ), could thus be recommended for specification of diffusion

## 5.2 PROGRESSIVE DISTILLATION

We evaluate our proposed progressive distillation algorithm on 4 data sets: CIFAR-10, 64 _×_ 64 downsampled ImageNet, 128 _×_ 128 LSUN bedrooms, and 128 _×_ 128 LSUN Church-Outdoor. For each data set we start by training a baseline model, after which we start the progressive distillation procedure. For CIFAR-10 we start progressive distillation from a teacher model taking 8192 steps. For the bigger data sets we start at 1024 steps. At every iteration of distillation we train for 50 thousand parameter updates, except for the distillation to 2 and 1 sampling steps, for which we use 100 thousand updates. We report FID results obtained after each iteration of the algorithm. Using these settings, the computational cost of progressive distillation to 4 sampling steps is comparable or less than for training the original model. In Appendix I we show that this computational cost can be reduce much further still, at a small cost in performance.

In Figure 4 we plot the resulting FID scores (Heusel et al., 2017) obtained for each number of sampling steps. We compare against the undistilled DDIM sampler, as well as to a highly optimized stochastic baseline sampler. For all four data sets, progressive distillation produces near optimal results up to 4 or 8 sampling steps. At 2 or 1 sampling steps, the sample quality degrades relatively more quickly. In contrast, the quality of the DDIM and stochastic samplers degrades very sharply after reducing the number of sampling steps below 128. Overall, we conclude that progressive distillation is thus an attractive solution for computational budgets that allow less than or equal to 128 sampling steps. Although our distillation procedure is designed for use with the DDIM sampler, the resulting distilled models can in principle also be used with stochastic sampling: we investigate this in Appendix F, and find that it achieves performance that falls in between the distilled DDIM sampler and the undistilled stochastic sampler.

Table 2 shows some of our results on CIFAR-10, and compares against other fast sampling methods in the literature: Our method compares favorably and attains higher sampling quality in fewer steps than most of the alternative methods. Figure 3 shows some random samples from our model obtained at different phases of the distillation process. Additional samples are provided in Appendix H.

Figure 4: Sample quality results as measured by FID for our distilled model on unconditional CIFAR-10, class-conditional 64x64 ImageNet, 128x128 LSUN bedrooms, and 128x128 LSUN church-outdoor. We compare against the DDIM sampler and against an optimized stochastic sampler, each evaluated using the same models that were used to initialize the progressive distillation procedure. For CIFAR-10 we report an average over 4 random seeds. For the other data sets we only use a single run because of their computational demand. For the stochastic sampler we set the variance as a log-scale interpolation between an upper and lower bound on the variance, following Nichol & Dhariwal (2021), but we use a single interpolation coefficient rather than a learned coefficient. We then tune this interpolation coefficient separately for each number of sampling steps and report only the best result for that number of steps: this way we obtained better results than with the learned interpolation.

that it requires constructing a large data set by running the original model at its full number of sampling steps: their cost of distillation thus scales linearly with this number of steps, which can be prohibitive. In contrast, our method never needs to run the original model at the full number of sampling steps: at every iteration of progressive distillation, the number of model evaluations is independent of the number of teacher sampling steps, allowing our method to scale up to large numbers of teacher steps at a logarithmic cost in total distillation time.

DDIM (Song et al., 2021a) was originally shown to be effective for few-step sampling, as was the probability flow sampler (Song et al., 2021c). Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2021) study fast SDE integrators for reverse diffusion processes, and Tzen & Raginsky (2019b) study unbiased samplers which may be useful for fast, high quality sampling as well.

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Other work on fast sampling can be viewed as manual or automated methods to adjust samplers or diffusion processes for fast generation. Nichol & Dhariwal (2021); Kong & Ping (2021) describe methods to adjust a discrete time diffusion model trained on many timesteps into models that can sample in few timesteps. Watson et al. (2021) describe a dynamic programming algorithm to reduce the number of timesteps for a diffusion model in a way that is optimal for log likelihood. Chen et al. (2021); Saharia et al. (2021); Ho et al. (2021) train diffusion models over continuous noise levels and tune samplers post training by adjusting the noise levels of a few-step discrete time reverse diffusion process. Their method is effective in highly conditioned settings such as text-to-speech and image super-resolution. San-Roman et al. (2021) train a new network to estimate the noise level of noisy data and show how to use this estimate to speed up sampling.

Alternative specifications of the diffusion model can also lend themselves to fast sampling, such as modified forward and reverse processes (Nachmani et al., 2021; Lam et al., 2021) and training diffusion models in latent space (Vahdat et al., 2021).

## 7 DISCUSSION

We have presented _progressive distillation_ , a method to drastically reduce the number of sampling steps required for high quality generation of images, and potentially other data, using diffusion models with deterministic samplers like DDIM (Song et al., 2020). By making these models cheaper to run at test time, we hope to increase their usefulness for practical applications, for which running time and computational requirements often represent important constraints.

In the current work we limited ourselves to setups where the student model has the same architecture and number of parameters as the teacher model: in future work we hope to relax this constraint and explore settings where the student model is smaller, potentially enabling further gains in test time computational requirements. In addition, we hope to move past the generation of images and also explore progressive distillation of diffusion models for different data modalities such as e.g. audio (Chen et al., 2021).

In addition to the proposed distillation procedure, some of our progress was realized through different parameterizations of the diffusion model and its training loss. We expect to see more progress in this direction as the community further explores this model class.

## REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENT

We provide full details on model architectures, training procedures, and hyperparameters in Appendix E, in addition to our discussion in Section 5. In Algorithm 2 we provide fairly detailed pseudocode that closely matches our actual implementation, which is available in open source at https: //github.com/google-research/google-research/tree/master/diffusion_distillation.

## ETHICS STATEMENT

In general, generative models can have unethical uses, such as fake content generation, and they can suffer from bias if applied to data sets that are not carefully curated. The focus of this paper specifically is on speeding up generative models at test time in order to reduce their computational demands; we do not have specific concerns with regards to this contribution.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper is characterized by exceptional clarity and technical rigor, addressing a critical bottleneck in the utility of diffusion models—the extreme computational cost of iterative sampling. The authors successfully bridge the gap between complex theoretical developments in generative modeling and practical application, introducing a novel, iterative "progressive distillation" technique that effectively reduces the required sampling steps by powers of two without a significant sacrifice in perceptual fidelity. The exposition is well-structured, logically scaffolding the reader from the motivation and background of diffusion and ODE-based sampling to the technical innovation of distillation. While the current text is highly accessible to researchers in machine learning, the most significant challenge for the paper lies in the empirical validation across broader datasets and the potential for a "quality ceiling" as the number of sampling steps approaches the extreme low end of the spectrum (e.g., 4 steps). Overall, the work represents a substantial contribution to the field, offering a highly efficient and elegant solution to one of the most prominent drawbacks of state-of-the-art generative architectures.

comments.explanation: If we define v ≡ αtϵ − σt x, then the clean data x is related to the noisy latent zt = αt x + σtϵ by ϵ = (zt - αt x) / σt. Substituting this into the definition of v: v = αt((zt - αt x) / σt) - σt x. Rearranging for x: v = (αt zt / σt) - (αt^2 / σt)x - σt x = (αt/ σt)zt - x(αt^2 + σt^2)/ σt. Since αt^2 + σt^2 = 1 (for variance-preserving processes), we get v = (αt / σt)zt - x / σt. Therefore, x = αt zt - σt v. comments.title: Loss weighting contradiction with claims
comments.quote: The setup of Ho et al. (2020) weights the reconstruction loss by the signal-to-noise ratio, implicitly gives a weight of zero to data with zero SNR, and is therefore not a suitable choice for distillation.
comments.explanation: The authors claim the original Ho et al. (2020) loss is not suitable for distillation because it gives zero weight at zero SNR. However, in section 2, they define the epsilon-parameterization objective where w(λt) = exp(λt). By definition, if λt is the log-SNR, then exp(λt) = SNR. The authors then propose alternatives, but in their own Section 4/Table 1, they test 'SNR' (which is the standard Ho et al weighting) and find it performs almost identically to their other options. If the standard weighting were genuinely 'not suitable' and 'unstable' for distillation as implied, the ablation results would likely show massive failure rather than competitive performance. The text is overly disparaging of existing methods to justify the novelty of the 'Truncated SNR' or 'SNR+1' choices.

comments.title: Potential Notation Conflict in $\mathbf{x}$ Parameterization
comments.quote: $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\mathbf{z}_{t''} - (\sigma_{t''}/\sigma_t)\mathbf{z}_t}{\alpha_{t''} - (\sigma_{t''}/\sigma_t)\alpha_t}$

Based on standard DDIM reversal, the update involves the denoising model output. Earlier in Section 2, the DDIM update rule is $z_s = \alpha_s \hat{\mathbf{x}}_\theta(z_t) + \sigma_s ((z_t - \alpha_t \hat{\mathbf{x}}_\theta(z_t)) / \sigma_t)$. The expression provided for $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ in Algorithm 2 appears to lack the model $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_\eta$ entirely, appearing to define the target $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ solely as a function of the noise-corrupted latents $\mathbf{z}_t$ and $\mathbf{z}_{t''}$. While the text mentions this is derived from 'inverting a single step of DDIM', the formula as written implies $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ is independent of the teacher model $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_\eta$, which contradicts the logic of distillation where $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ must be the target derived from the teacher's prediction. comments.title: Ambiguity in loss weighting notation
comments.quote: weighting function $w(\lambda_t) = \exp(\lambda_t)$
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the authors state $w(\lambda_t) = \exp(\lambda_t)$ for $\epsilon$-prediction. progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper introduces a highly promising method for accelerating diffusion model inference via progressive distillation, effectively addressing the critical limitation of slow sampling times. The proposed strategy of iteratively distilling a deterministic $N$-step sampler into a half-step model is conceptually sound and efficiently amortizes the integration cost, as evidenced by the impressive results summarized in the abstract, such as achieving an FID of 3.0 on CIFAR-10 with only 4 steps while maintaining quality comparable to thousands of steps. The technical quality appears strong, and the claim that the full distillation procedure incurs no additional time cost relative to the original model training is a significant efficiency advantage. However, the manuscript suffers from notable clarity and presentation issues in the provided excerpt; specifically, Section 2 contains severe text corruption and parsing errors (e.g., 'q( **z** _|_ **x** ) into an estimatethis to keep our'), which disrupt the flow and hinder comprehension. Furthermore, while the abstract highlights 'new parameterizations' as a key contribution to stability, these details are not elaborated upon in the introduction, leaving the precise mechanism for handling few-step instability unclear at this stage. The authors should prioritize correcting the text artifacts and providing a more detailed preliminary exposition of the parameterizations to ensure the methodological rigor is transparent.

comments.title: Contradictory phrasing on discrete vs. continuous time training
comments.quote: Unlike our procedure for training the original model, we always run progressive distillation in discrete time: we sample this discrete time such that the highest time index corresponds to a signal-to-noise ratio of zero, i.e. _α_ 1 = 0, which exactly matches the distribution of input noise **z** 1 _∼N_ ( **0** _,_ **I** ) that is used at test time. We found this to work slightly better than starting from a non-zero signalto-noise ratio as used by e.g. Ho et al. (2020), both for training the original model as well as when performing progressive distillation.
comments.explanation: The opening clause states a difference between the procedure for training the original model and progressive distillation ('Unlike our procedure for training the original model...'). However, the closing clause explicitly states that this discrete-time setup with α₁=0 is used 'both for training the original model as well as when performing progressive distillation.' This creates a direct logical contradiction. The authors likely meant to contrast their approach with the continuous-time formulation introduced earlier in the section, or with other works that do not use α₁=0. A careful reader could be confused about whether the original model was actually trained differently or not.

comments.title: Ambiguous terminology regarding 'teacher steps' in target calculation
comments.quote: We calculate this target value by running 2 DDIM sampling steps using the teacher, starting from **z** _t_ and ending at **z** _t−_ 1 _/N_ , with _N_ being the number of student sampling steps.
comments.explanation: The text describes generating the target by 'running 2 DDIM sampling steps using the teacher.' However, the teacher model is typically trained and evaluated with a much larger number of steps (e.g., M=1000 or 8192), making a single teacher step correspond to a time interval of 1/M, not 1/N. The accompanying Algorithm 2 clarifies that they actually use the teacher's network to perform two sub-steps of size 0.5/N over the student's coarser time grid. Phrasing it as '2 teacher DDIM steps' is misleading, as it conflates the teacher's native step size with the student's timeline. It would be clearer to state that they evaluate the teacher's transition over a time interval of 1/N using two sub-steps, or that they 'amortize' two student steps into the teacher's dynamics.

comments.title: Missing mathematical definitions for proposed loss weightings
comments.quote:
We consider two alternative training loss weightings:

We tried the following options, and found all to work well with progressive distillation:

We examine both choices in our ablation study in Section 5.1, and find both to be good choices for training diffusion models.
comments.explanation: The manuscript explicitly announces 'two alternative training loss weightings' in multiple passages but immediately cuts to lists of parameterizations or ablation results without providing the actual mathematical formulations. While Table 1 and Figure 2 reference these as 'SNR+1' and 'Truncated SNR', the exact functional forms governing the loss modification are completely absent. Without these equations, readers cannot accurately reproduce the training procedure or understand the weightings' behavior at the boundaries. This is likely a dropped formula block due to formatting or OCR, but it critically underspecifies the methodology.

comments.title: Notation collision for γ between noise hyperparameter and learning rate
comments.quote:
where **ϵ** is standard Gaussian noise, and **γ** is a hyperparameter that controls how much noise is added during sampling, following Nichol & Dhariwal (2021).

...|_θ ←θ −γ∇θLθ_|_▷_Optimization|_θ ←θ −γ∇θLθ_|
comments.explanation: The symbol γ is reused for two fundamentally different quantities in the paper. In Section 2, γ is explicitly defined as a hyperparameter controlling the noise variance added during ancestral sampling. However, in both Algorithm 1 and Algorithm 2, γ is used as the learning rate step size for gradient descent (θ ← θ − γ∇θLθ). This collision creates ambiguity for readers trying to implement the algorithm or follow the mathematical derivations, as γ appears in both the probabilistic formulation of the sampler and the optimization routine. It would be clearer to rename one of these (e.g., γ_noise for the sampling parameter and ρ or η for the learning rate).

comments.title: Loose terminology for combined x/ε prediction interpolation
comments.quote: merging the predictions via **x** = _σt_[2] **[x]**[˜] _[θ]_[(] **[z]** _[t]_[)][+] _[α][t]_[(] **[z]** _[t][−][σ][t][ϵ]_[˜] _[θ]_[(] **[z]** _[t]_[))][,][thus] smoothly interpolating between predicting **x** directly and predicting via _ϵ_ .
comments.explanation: The authors propose a linear combination $\mathbf{x} = \sigma_t^2 \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_\theta + \alpha_t(\mathbf{z}_t - \sigma_t \tilde{\boldsymbol{\epsilon}}_\theta)$ to merge network outputs. When predictions are consistent with the noise schedule ($\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_\theta \approx \mathbf{x}$ and $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\epsilon}}_\theta \approx \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$), this formula exactly recovers $\mathbf{x}$ due to the variance-preserving condition $\alpha_t^2 + \sigma_t^2 = 1$. Mathematically, this is a sound projection. However, describing it as 'smoothly interpolating between predicting **x** directly and predicting via $\epsilon$' is slightly misleading. The formula does not introduce a tunable interpolation parameter; rather, it is a fixed, schedule-dependent blend that reduces to $\mathbf{x}$ under consistency. Clarifying that it is a deterministic fusion rule derived from the diffusion prior would improve precision without changing the method.

comments.title: Attribution of SNR-weighting to Ho et al. (2020) may cause confusion
comments.quote: The setup of Ho et al. (2020) weights the reconstruction loss by the signal-to-noise ratio, implicitly gives a weight of zero to data with zero SNR, and is therefore not a suitable choice for distillation.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly note earlier in the section that minimizing constant MSE in $\epsilon$-space is mathematically equivalent to SNR-weighted MSE in $\mathbf{x}$-space (weight $w(\lambda_t) = \exp(\lambda_t)$). However, attributing the SNR weighting directly to 'the setup of Ho et al. (2020)' is potentially confusing. Ho et al. (2020) explicitly formulates and trains using unit weight in $\epsilon$-space; the SNR weighting is an *implicit* consequence of the coordinate change, not the original formulation or intent of the paper. Readers familiar with DDPM will see a constant $\epsilon$-loss and may initially question the claim. Acknowledging that the SNR weighting is a mathematical equivalence in $\mathbf{x}$-space rather than the original training objective would improve clarity.

comments.title: Misleading complexity claim regarding distillation cost
comments.quote: allowing our method to scale up to large numbers of teacher steps at a logarithmic cost in total distillation time.
comments.explanation: The authors claim the total distillation time scales logarithmically with the number of teacher steps. However, progressive distillation iteratively halves the student steps, requiring the teacher to run for $N$, then $N/2$, then $N/4$ steps, etc. The sum is $\sum_{k=0}^{\log N} N/2^k = 2N$, meaning the total computational cost scales *linearly* with the initial number of teacher steps (specifically, bounded by at most $2\times$ the original sampling cost). The number of *iterations* is logarithmic, but framing the total time cost as logarithmic overstates the efficiency gain and could mislead readers about scaling behavior.

comments.title: Likely numerical typo in Table 2 comparison
comments.quote: Dynamic step-size extrapolation + VP-deep|48|82.42
comments.explanation: The reported FID of 82.42 for the dynamic step-size extrapolation method (Jolicoeur-Martineau et al., 2021) is drastically higher than the typical results reported for this approach on CIFAR-10, which usually fall in the 2–4 FID range. This is almost certainly a decimal placement error in the original paper (likely intended to be ~2.42 or ~8.242, though 2.42 is standard). As written, it artificially inflates the performance gap between Progressive Distillation and this baseline, making the comparison misleading.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper introduces a highly promising method for accelerating diffusion model inference via progressive distillation, effectively addressing the critical limitation of slow sampling times. The proposed strategy of iteratively distilling a deterministic $N$-step sampler into a half-step model is conceptually sound and efficiently amortizes the integration cost, as evidenced by the impressive results summarized in the abstract, such as achieving an FID of 3.0 on CIFAR-10 with only 4 steps while maintaining quality comparable to thousands of steps. The technical quality appears strong, and the claim that the full distillation procedure incurs no additional time cost relative to the original model training is a significant efficiency advantage. However, the manuscript suffers from notable clarity and presentation issues in the provided excerpt; specifically, Section 2 contains severe text corruption and parsing errors (e.g., 'q( **z** _|_ **x** ) into an estimatethis to keep our'), which disrupt the flow and hinder comprehension. Furthermore, while the abstract highlights 'new parameterizations' as a key contribution to stability, these details are not elaborated upon in the introduction, leaving the precise mechanism for handling few-step instability unclear at this stage. The authors should prioritize correcting the text artifacts and providing a more detailed preliminary exposition of the parameterizations to ensure the methodological rigor is transparent.

comments.title: Contradictory phrasing on discrete vs. continuous time training
comments.quote: Unlike our procedure for training the original model, we always run progressive distillation in discrete time: we sample this discrete time such that the highest time index corresponds to a signal-to-noise ratio of zero, i.e. _α_ 1 = 0, which exactly matches the distribution of input noise **z** 1 _∼N_ ( **0** _,_ **I** ) that is used at test time. We found this to work slightly better than starting from a non-zero signalto-noise ratio as used by e.g. Ho et al. (2020), both for training the original model as well as when performing progressive distillation.
comments.explanation: The opening clause states a difference between the procedure for training the original model and progressive distillation ('Unlike our procedure for training the original model...'). However, the closing clause explicitly states that this discrete-time setup with α₁=0 is used 'both for training the original model as well as when performing progressive distillation.' This creates a direct logical contradiction. The authors likely meant to contrast their approach with the continuous-time formulation introduced earlier in the section, or with other works that do not use α₁=0. A careful reader could be confused about whether the original model was actually trained differently or not.

comments.title: Ambiguous terminology regarding 'teacher steps' in target calculation
comments.quote: We calculate this target value by running 2 DDIM sampling steps using the teacher, starting from **z** _t_ and ending at **z** _t−_ 1 _/N_ , with _N_ being the number of student sampling steps.
comments.explanation: The text describes generating the target by 'running 2 DDIM sampling steps using the teacher.' However, the teacher model is typically trained and evaluated with a much larger number of steps (e.g., M=1000 or 8192), making a single teacher step correspond to a time interval of 1/M, not 1/N. The accompanying Algorithm 2 clarifies that they actually use the teacher's network to perform two sub-steps of size 0.5/N over the student's coarser time grid. Phrasing it as '2 teacher DDIM steps' is misleading, as it conflates the teacher's native step size with the student's timeline. It would be clearer to state that they evaluate the teacher's transition over a time interval of 1/N using two sub-steps, or that they 'amortize' two student steps into the teacher's dynamics.

The actual mathematical definitions or names of the two weightings are absent in this passage (though they later appear as 'SNR+1' and 'Truncated SNR' in Table 1). comments.title: Notation collision for γ between noise hyperparameter and learning rate
comments.quote:
where **ϵ** is standard Gaussian noise, and **γ** is a hyperparameter that controls how much noise is added during sampling, following Nichol & Dhariwal (2021).

...|_θ ←θ −γ∇θLθ_|_▷_Optimization|_θ ←θ −γ∇θLθ_|
comments.explanation: The symbol γ is reused for two fundamentally different quantities in the paper. In Section 2, γ is explicitly defined as a hyperparameter controlling the noise variance added during ancestral sampling. However, in both Algorithm 1 and Algorithm 2, γ is used as the learning rate step size for gradient descent (θ ← θ − γ∇θLθ). This collision creates ambiguity for readers trying to implement the algorithm or follow the mathematical derivations, as γ appears in both the probabilistic formulation of the sampler and the optimization routine. It would be clearer to rename one of these (e.g., γ_noise for the sampling parameter and ρ or η for the learning rate).

comments.title: Missing mathematical definitions for alternative loss weightings

We consider two alternative training loss weightings:

We examine both choices in our ablation study in Section 5.1, and find both to be good choices for training diffusion models.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly announces two alternative training loss weightings but immediately jumps to discussing the ablation results without providing their functional forms. While Figure 2 and Table 1 reference 'Truncated SNR' and 'SNR+1' weightings, and standard literature suggests these likely correspond to formulations like $w(\lambda_t) = \max(\exp(\lambda_t), \tau)$ or $w(\lambda_t) = \exp(\lambda_t) + 1$, the exact equations are completely absent from the provided passage. Without these formulas, a reader cannot accurately reproduce the training procedure or understand how the weightings behave at the boundaries (e.g., as $\lambda_t \to -\infty$). Given the OCR context, this is likely a dropped formula block, but as presented, it leaves a critical methodological detail underspecified.

comments.title: Loose terminology for combined x/ε prediction interpolation
comments.quote: merging the predictions via **x** = _σt_[2] **[x]**[˜] _[θ]_[(] **[z]** _[t]_[)][+] _[α][t]_[(] **[z]** _[t][−][σ][t][ϵ]_[˜] _[θ]_[(] **[z]** _[t]_[))][,][thus] smoothly interpolating between predicting **x** directly and predicting via _ϵ_ .
comments.explanation: The authors propose a linear combination $\mathbf{x} = \sigma_t^2 \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_\theta + \alpha_t(\mathbf{z}_t - \sigma_t \tilde{\boldsymbol{\epsilon}}_\theta)$ to merge network outputs. When predictions are consistent with the noise schedule ($\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_\theta \approx \mathbf{x}$ and $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\epsilon}}_\theta \approx \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$), this formula exactly recovers $\mathbf{x}$ due to the variance-preserving condition $\alpha_t^2 + \sigma_t^2 = 1$. Mathematically, this is a sound projection. However, describing it as 'smoothly interpolating between predicting **x** directly and predicting via $\epsilon$' is slightly misleading. The formula does not introduce a tunable interpolation parameter; rather, it is a fixed, schedule-dependent blend that reduces to $\mathbf{x}$ under consistency. Clarifying that it is a deterministic fusion rule derived from the diffusion prior would improve precision without changing the method.

comments.title: Attribution of SNR-weighting to Ho et al. (2020) may cause confusion
comments.quote: The setup of Ho et al. (2020) weights the reconstruction loss by the signal-to-noise ratio, implicitly gives a weight of zero to data with zero SNR, and is therefore not a suitable choice for distillation.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly note earlier in the section that minimizing constant MSE in $\epsilon$-space is mathematically equivalent to SNR-weighted MSE in $\mathbf{x}$-space (weight $w(\lambda_t) = \exp(\lambda_t)$). However, attributing the SNR weighting directly to 'the setup of Ho et al. (2020)' is potentially confusing. Ho et al. (2020) explicitly formulates and trains using unit weight in $\epsilon$-space; the SNR weighting is an *implicit* consequence of the coordinate change, not the original formulation or intent of the paper. Readers familiar with DDPM will see a constant $\epsilon$-loss and may initially question the claim. Acknowledging that the SNR weighting is a mathematical equivalence in $\mathbf{x}$-space rather than the original training objective would improve clarity.

comments.quote: This is without loss of generalization, as shown by [(Kingma et al., 2021,] appendix G):
comments.explanation: The standard mathematical convention is "without loss of generality" (WLOG). "Without loss of generalization" is a common malapropism that, while understandable in context, is imprecise terminology for a theoretical methods section. progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound approach to accelerating diffusion model sampling through progressive distillation, building on the DDIM deterministic sampler. The clarity of the exposition is generally strong, with a clear problem statement, a concise background section, and a intuitive figure illustrating the distillation process. However, the paper's reliance on the deterministic probability flow ODE and DDIM as the base sampler raises a significant issue: the distillation procedure is inherently tied to this specific sampling trajectory, which may limit generalization to other samplers (e.g., stochastic ancestral sampling or advanced ODE solvers) that could offer better quality-speed trade-offs. Additionally, the claim that progressive distillation does not exceed the original training time requires careful scrutiny, as the repeated distillation steps involve retraining the model on generated data, which could accumulate computational cost and potential error propagation; the paper should more clearly quantify the total training overhead relative to the initial model. The introduction of new parameterizations for stability is a valuable contribution, but the description of these parameterizations is deferred to later sections, making it hard to assess their novelty and efficacy from the provided excerpt. Overall, the paper makes a compelling case for its core idea, but its impact depends on rigorous validation of the distillation's scalability to larger datasets and more diverse tasks, as well as a clearer discussion of limitations regarding the deterministic sampling assumption.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper addresses the critical bottleneck of slow sampling in diffusion models through an innovative "progressive distillation" framework, effectively amortizing the integration of the probability flow ODE into a compact model. The writing style is clear and concise, establishing a strong motivation in the introduction by highlighting the disparity between diffusion's superior density estimation capabilities and its slow inference speed compared to GANs. The background section is well-structured, providing precise mathematical definitions for the forward/reverse diffusion processes and the ODE-based samplers. A significant issue regarding the input text provided is that it cuts off abruptly in the middle of the background section, precluding a full evaluation of the core distillation algorithm and experimental validation. Furthermore, the text contains formatting artifacts and garbled mathematical symbols (e.g., repeated header text and broken LaTeX markup) that require correction to ensure a clean presentation.

comments.title: Questionable Performance Claim Regarding LSGM
comments.quote: Our method compares favorably and attains higher sampling quality in fewer steps than most of the alternative methods.
comments.explanation: The text asserts that Progressive Distillation (PD) attains 'higher sampling quality' (implying a lower FID score, as is standard in the field) than 'most' alternative methods. However, Table 2 explicitly compares PD against LSGM (Vahdat et al., 2021). LSGM achieves a FID of 2.10 at 138 sampling steps, while PD achieves 3.00 at 4 steps. Since 2.10 is strictly less than 3.00, LSGM possesses higher quality (lower FID) results. Claiming PD has higher quality than 'most' methods when one primary competitor in the table objectively performs better is an overstatement. Unless the authors implicitly exclude LSGM from 'alternative methods' (e.g., due to it being a latent space model), this statement contradicts the data presented in the same table.
